summary:
The development of tin bronze trade networks approximately 2500–2000 BC represents one of the most important commercial and technological transformations of the ancient world. Bronze — an alloy of copper and tin — is significantly harder and more durable than pure copper, making it superior for weapons, tools, and prestige objects. However, while copper is relatively widely distributed geographically, tin is rare and concentrated in a few geological deposits. This scarcity created the conditions for long-distance trade networks of extraordinary reach, connecting tin sources in distant regions to the copper-rich civilisations of the ancient world.

The major tin sources of the Bronze Age were located in Anatolia, Cornwall (Britain), the Erzgebirge mountains of central Europe, Central Asia (possibly including parts of modern Afghanistan and Uzbekistan), and possibly West Africa. The Persian Gulf and eastern Mediterranean routes connected Afghan tin to Mesopotamian copper-working centres; British and European tin reached the Mediterranean through exchange networks across continental Europe. Textual evidence from the later Old Assyrian period (around 1900 BC) documents the large-scale tin trade between Assur in Mesopotamia and Anatolian markets, providing a glimpse of how these networks functioned.

The social and political consequences of Bronze Age tin trade were profound. Polities positioned astride the tin trade routes could accumulate considerable wealth as commercial intermediaries. The control of metal trade became a significant source of political power, contributing to the rise of warrior aristocracies and palatial states. The mutual dependence created by the separation of copper and tin sources — requiring exchange across hundreds or thousands of kilometres — bound the civilisations of the ancient world into an integrated economic system and made the stability of that system a matter of existential importance for societies dependent on bronze for military and agricultural technology.
